statute: N.C.G.S. § 87-1 et seq. (General Contractor Licensing); N.C.G.S. § 44A (Mechanics Lien Law)
license_notes: NC General Contractor license required for projects of $40,000 or more. Residential Contractor specialty license covers projects under $40,000 for residential work.

mandatory_clauses.label: Contractor license disclosure
Lien agent notice
Mechanics lien notice
mandatory_clauses.text:
NOTICE OF LIEN RIGHTS

Under North Carolina law (N.C.G.S. § 44A), contractors, subcontractors, and material suppliers who provide labor or materials for your property improvement may file a lien against your property if they are not paid. Even if you pay your contractor in full, unpaid subcontractors and suppliers may still have lien rights if they timely notified the lien agent (for projects of $30,000 or more). Protect yourself by verifying all parties are paid and obtaining written lien waivers.

mandatory_clauses.label: Federal 3-day right to cancel (TILA)

penalties_for_non_compliance: Contracting without license over threshold = criminal offense (misdemeanor). Contract may be unenforceable. License board disciplinary action.
notes: North Carolina requires a General Contractor license for projects of $40,000 or more. Residential projects under $40,000 may use a Residential Contractor specialty license. CRITICAL: For projects $30,000 or more, the property owner must designate a lien agent before work begins (N.C.G.S. § 44A-11.1). Failure to designate a lien agent can affect lien rights. Federal 3-day notice satisfies state cancellation requirements. NC has contractor license reciprocity with Georgia, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Tennessee.
